checked_evidence_span: NM report identifies Oropsylla montana as the most important plague vector for humans in New Mexico; USGS plague circular corroborates flea-borne vector context.
caveat: New Mexico / North America public-health context; not a universal global primary vector claim.
decision_reason: Direct named-vector support for O. montana with geographic caveat.

checked_evidence_span: CDC WNV guideline lists Culex quinquefasciatus as a primary WNV vector in southern states and among the three major WNV vectors.
caveat: United States regional vector framing.
decision_reason: Direct official guideline support.

checked_evidence_span: CDC WNV guideline lists Culex tarsalis as a primary WNV vector in western states and among the three major WNV vectors.
caveat: United States regional vector framing.
decision_reason: Direct official guideline support.

checked_evidence_span: Abreu et al. identify Haemagogus janthinomys and Hg. leucocelaenus as primary vectors in the Brazil 2016-2018 yellow fever outbreak based on distribution, abundance, and natural infection rates.
caveat: Brazil Atlantic Forest outbreak context; supports sylvatic/outbreak vector role.
decision_reason: Direct species-level natural infection and outbreak-vector support.

checked_evidence_span: Abreu et al. identify Haemagogus leucocelaenus and Hg. janthinomys as primary vectors in the Brazil 2016-2018 yellow fever outbreak based on distribution, abundance, and natural infection rates.
caveat: Brazil Atlantic Forest outbreak context; supports sylvatic/outbreak vector role.
decision_reason: Direct species-level natural infection and outbreak-vector support.

checked_evidence_span: RVF review states cattle, sheep, goats, and camels are susceptible and serve as amplifying hosts; later section calls cattle, sheep, and goats the most important amplifying hosts.
caveat: Species-level row derived from livestock statement; goat-specific support is explicit but not a controlled species-only study.
decision_reason: Direct review support for goat as part of named amplifying livestock set.

checked_evidence_span: RVF review states cattle, sheep, goats, and camels are susceptible and serve as amplifying hosts; later section calls cattle, sheep, and goats the most important amplifying hosts.
caveat: Species-level row derived from livestock statement; sheep-specific support is explicit and strongest among livestock.
decision_reason: Direct review support for sheep as part of named amplifying livestock set.

checked_evidence_span: ECDC states Hyalomma ticks, mainly H. marginatum, H. anatolicum, H. rufipes and H. asiaticum, are competent CCHFV vectors/reservoirs, with H. marginatum principal in Europe.
caveat: Europe-heavy official source; H. marginatum is explicitly principal in Europe.
decision_reason: Direct official species-level support.

checked_evidence_span: WHO/CDC support wildlife-to-human spillover followed by human-to-human Ebola transmission via body fluids.
checked_evidence_location: WHO Ebola fact sheet lines 287-289; CDC Ebola spread page lines 117-124
caveat: The candidate role spillover_host is not ideal for Homo sapiens; evidence supports human outbreak amplification after spillover.
decision_reason: Keep as evidence for human amplification, not as an exact spillover_host import.

checked_evidence_span: WHO states Marburg is transmitted from fruit bats to people and then spreads through human-to-human transmission; CDC similarly describes spillover from bats followed by person-to-person spread.
checked_evidence_location: WHO Marburg fact sheet lines 219, 230-233; CDC Marburg about lines 140-147
caveat: The candidate role spillover_host is not ideal for Homo sapiens; evidence supports human outbreak amplification after spillover.
decision_reason: Keep as evidence for human amplification, not as an exact spillover_host import.

checked_evidence_span: WHO Nipah guidance documents animal-to-human risk and measures to reduce human-to-human transmission.
caveat: The candidate role spillover_host is not ideal for Homo sapiens; human-to-human transmission is context-dependent.
decision_reason: Useful evidence, but role should be reframed as human outbreak amplification if imported.

checked_evidence_span: Chua et al. isolated Nipah virus from Pteropus hypomelanus urine/fruit swab material and state this corroborates the species as one of the natural hosts.
caveat: Malaysia/Island flying-fox context.
decision_reason: Direct live-virus isolation from species-level bat source.

checked_evidence_span: Pteropus vampyrus evidence includes isolation/characterization from naturally infected bats and serological/recrudescence evidence in captive P. vampyrus.
caveat: Malaysia P. vampyrus context.
decision_reason: Direct species-level natural infection support.

checked_evidence_span: WHO states Lassa is primarily transmitted from Mastomys-contaminated materials and that person-to-person transmission can occur, especially in healthcare settings.
caveat: The candidate role spillover_host is not ideal for Homo sapiens; evidence supports context-dependent person-to-person transmission.
decision_reason: Useful evidence, but import should use amplification/secondary transmission wording if at all.

checked_evidence_span: Diallo et al. review supports Aedes furcifer among African dengue vectors and sylvatic-cycle context.
caveat: Review-based African sylvatic vector row; not a global urban vector claim.
decision_reason: Source-backed candidate row with regional caveat.

checked_evidence_span: Candidate primary dengue paper was not locally fetched; CHIKV Yap paper cites Ae. hensilli implication as dengue epidemic vector on Yap/Palau and notes weaker dengue support than CHIKV/Zika.
caveat: Need the Savage 1998 dengue paper text before import; current local evidence is secondary and says dengue support is weaker.
decision_reason: Do not import until primary dengue source is checked.

checked_evidence_span: Diallo et al. review supports Aedes luteocephalus among African dengue vectors and sylvatic-cycle context.
caveat: Review-based African sylvatic vector row; not a global urban vector claim.
decision_reason: Source-backed candidate row with regional caveat.

checked_evidence_span: Rosen demonstrates Aedes polynesiensis can transmit dengue experimentally and discusses epidemiologic observations suggesting it as a natural/important vector in Polynesia.
caveat: Old but direct experimental/epidemiological evidence; Polynesia context.
decision_reason: Direct species-level support.

checked_evidence_span: Diallo et al. review supports Aedes taylori among African dengue vectors and sylvatic-cycle context.
caveat: Review-based African sylvatic vector row; not a global urban vector claim.
decision_reason: Source-backed candidate row with regional caveat.

checked_evidence_span: Seery et al. describe black-tailed prairie dogs as major amplifying hosts for Yersinia pestis and document high mortality epizootic context.
caveat: North American prairie-dog epizootic role; not a universal plague reservoir claim.
decision_reason: Direct species-level amplifying-host wording.

checked_evidence_span: Miarinjara et al. and supporting literature describe Oropsylla hirsuta as an abundant prairie dog flea vector and support its role in prairie dog plague epizootics.
caveat: Prairie-dog epizootic context; keep candidate rather than main/primary.
decision_reason: Good species-level candidate vector support.

checked_evidence_span: CDC WNV guideline names American robin/Turdus migratorius as an amplifier host example in many locations.
caveat: Species name in guideline has OCR typo; map to Turdus migratorius with review note.
decision_reason: Official named-host amplifier evidence.

checked_evidence_span: CDC WNV guideline describes Culex salinarius as an important enzootic and epidemic vector in the northeastern United States.
caveat: Northeastern United States context; not a primary vector nationally.
decision_reason: Direct official regional vector support.

checked_evidence_span: Yap CHIKV study detected multiple positive Ae. hensilli pools, argues Ae. hensilli was more important than Ae. aegypti on Yap, and explicitly incriminates it as an epidemic vector.
caveat: Yap outbreak context; not a global CHIKV vector claim.
decision_reason: Direct species-level outbreak/vector evidence.

checked_evidence_span: RVF review states cattle, sheep, and goats are the most important amplifying hosts; cattle are also susceptible and important in regional livestock-density risk.
caveat: Cattle are important amplifying livestock but generally less susceptible to clinical disease than sheep/goats.
decision_reason: Direct review support for cattle as part of named amplifying livestock set.

checked_evidence_span: RVF sources identify Aedes mcintoshi among primary floodwater Aedes vectors and describe infected Aedes eggs/maintenance-cycle ecology.
caveat: Review-based species row; maintenance wording is ecological synthesis.
decision_reason: Good source-backed maintenance-vector support.

checked_evidence_span: RVF sources list Culex poicilipes among RVFV vectors and describe Culex secondary vectors transmitting from highly viraemic animals to humans.
caveat: Best interpreted as secondary/epidemic vector rather than maintenance vector.
decision_reason: Source-backed regional epidemic vector support.

checked_evidence_span: Ortiz/Weaver study reports high susceptibility and transmission potential of Psorophora confinnis and concludes it is an important bridge vector for enzootic and epizootic VEEV.
caveat: Northern South America / VEEV subtype context; bridge-vector role is supported but regional.
decision_reason: Direct species-level competence and bridge-vector support.

checked_evidence_span: ECDC states Hyalomma ticks, mainly H. marginatum, H. anatolicum, H. rufipes and H. asiaticum, are competent CCHFV vectors/reservoirs.
caveat: Species is named as competent vector/reservoir; main-vector strength is less Europe-specific than H. marginatum.
decision_reason: Direct official species-level support.

checked_evidence_span: ECDC states CCHFV was detected in H. lusitanicum in Spain before the first human case and that this species seems important in virus circulation there.
caveat: Spain-specific circulation role; do not generalize globally.
decision_reason: Official regional support for virus-circulation role.

checked_evidence_span: ECDC states Hyalomma ticks, mainly H. marginatum, H. anatolicum, H. rufipes and H. asiaticum, are competent CCHFV vectors/reservoirs.
caveat: Species is named as competent vector/reservoir; regional importance varies.
decision_reason: Direct official species-level support.

checked_evidence_span: Systematic review finds Culicoides sonorensis and C. paraensis consistently demonstrate high competence to transmit OROV, but emphasizes current outbreak vectors remain uncertain.
caveat: Competence evidence only; no evidence that C. sonorensis is a field vector in current outbreaks.
decision_reason: Strong competence-only support.

checked_evidence_span: Zhao et al. report E. amurensis hedgehogs with high SFTSV seroprevalence/RNA prevalence, robust viremias, completed tick-host cycle, and conclude hedgehogs are notable wildlife amplifying hosts.
caveat: China/endemic-area hedgehog evidence; not a global host claim.
decision_reason: Direct species-level amplifying-host support.

checked_evidence_span: Fang et al. demonstrate H. flava can acquire SFTSV and transmit it through stages and to rabbits; they conclude H. flava is a competent vector/reservoir.
caveat: Competence/experimental transmission support; field importance remains context-dependent.
decision_reason: Direct experimental competence support.

checked_evidence_span: Hu et al. show I. sinensis transovarial transmission, adult tick-to-mouse transmission, and conclude it can play roles in maintaining/transmitting SFTSV.
caveat: Evidence is experimental and geographically bounded; keep candidate rather than main vector.
decision_reason: Direct experimental candidate-vector support.

checked_evidence_span: TBEV reservoir-host review and mouse infection paper support Apodemus flavicollis as a species-level small-rodent host in the TBEV transmission cycle.
caveat: Review plus experimental support; reservoir role is shared with broader small rodent community.
decision_reason: Species-level TBEV host support is sufficient for candidate import.

checked_evidence_span: TBEV reservoir-host review supports Myodes glareolus/bank vole as a species-level small-rodent reservoir host.
caveat: Review-based support; reservoir role is shared with broader small rodent community.
decision_reason: Species-level TBEV host support is sufficient for candidate import.

checked_evidence_span: Dermacentor reticulatus study demonstrates infected ticks transmit TBEV to mice and concludes D. reticulatus is a biologically effective, underrecognized TBEV vector.
caveat: Non-core/underrecognized vector; do not upgrade to main vector.
decision_reason: Direct experimental vector support.

checked_evidence_span: TBEV review states Haemaphysalis concinna is one of the major vectors for TBEV transmission in Asia.
caveat: Review-based regional Asia vector row; candidate status is safer than main vector.
decision_reason: Source-backed non-core candidate vector support.

checked_evidence_span: Walsh et al. describe Ebola Zaire introductions into humans when people handled infected gorilla/chimpanzee carcasses during ape die-offs.
caveat: Great apes are spillover/source animals in documented chains, not reservoir hosts.
decision_reason: Direct wildlife source/species evidence with non-reservoir caveat.

checked_evidence_span: Walsh et al. describe Ebola Zaire introductions into humans when people handled infected gorilla/chimpanzee carcasses during ape die-offs.
caveat: Great apes are spillover/source animals in documented chains, not reservoir hosts.
decision_reason: Direct wildlife source/species evidence with non-reservoir caveat.

checked_evidence_span: WHO Marburg fact sheet states first recognized outbreaks were associated with laboratory work using African green monkeys imported from Uganda.
caveat: Historical/laboratory-associated source-animal role; not reservoir evidence.
decision_reason: Official historical source-animal support.

checked_evidence_span: Pteropus lylei sources report Nipah virus in Lyle's flying foxes and molecular characterization from Thai fruit bats.
caveat: Cambodia/Thailand species-level support; add alongside existing Pteropus spp. group with caveat.
decision_reason: Reasonable species-level reservoir support.

checked_evidence_span: Kerala sources report Nipah sequences/detection in Pteropus medius during human outbreaks.
caveat: India/Kerala species-level evidence; add alongside existing Pteropus spp. group with caveat.
decision_reason: Reasonable species-level reservoir support.

checked_evidence_span: WHO 2025 Sudan virus DON documents confirmed human cases, contact-linked secondary cases, and ongoing investigation of the outbreak source.
caveat: The animal source is unresolved; Homo sapiens evidence supports outbreak transmission rather than spillover_host as a role.
decision_reason: Useful human outbreak evidence but exact candidate role should be reframed.

checked_evidence_span: Primary field study strongly identifies C. musculinus as principal Junin reservoir and detects C. laucha rarely; Canada sheet lists C. laucha among natural hosts and principal reservoirs.
caveat: Evidence for C. laucha is weaker than for C. musculinus; treat as possible/minor reservoir candidate, not equal primary reservoir.
decision_reason: Do not import as medium reservoir_host without manual review; evidence supports only a caveated candidate.

checked_evidence_span: Olayemi et al. report LASV in Hylomyscus pamfi and conclude H. pamfi and M. erythroleucus are full-fledged reservoirs; later review lists H. pamfi among four LASV reservoir species.
caveat: Newer/additional reservoir species; region-specific and less canonical than M. natalensis.
decision_reason: Direct species-level reservoir support.

checked_evidence_span: Olayemi et al. report LASV in Mastomys erythroleucus and conclude H. pamfi and M. erythroleucus are full-fledged reservoirs; later review lists M. erythroleucus among four LASV reservoir species.
caveat: Additional reservoir species; region-specific and less canonical than M. natalensis.
decision_reason: Direct species-level reservoir support.

checked_evidence_span: WHO reports 2024 A(H5N1) outbreaks in US dairy cattle; Nature/mbio sources document HPAI H5N1 in dairy cattle and cattle-to-cattle/milking-related spread.
checked_evidence_location: WHO influenza fact sheet lines 247-251; s41586-024-07766-6.txt lines 29-37; mostafa-et-al-2024...txt lines 287-295
caveat: Candidate proposed spillover_host, but current repo convention already uses susceptible_host_only for Bos taurus H5N1; avoid implying reservoir/amplifier without review.
decision_reason: Evidence is strong for cattle infection/outbreak host, but role should be conservative.

checked_evidence_span: Cat review states domestic cats and large felids are naturally and experimentally susceptible to HPAI H5N1; infections mostly follow contact with infected birds/raw poultry and cat-to-cat transmission may occur.
caveat: Susceptible host evidence only; role in broader epidemiology remains uncertain.
decision_reason: Direct susceptible-host support.

checked_evidence_span: Nature 2025 report and review support MPXV transmission from fire-footed rope squirrels to sooty mangabeys, with mangabeys as affected/spillover primates rather than reservoirs.
caveat: Single-chain/specific-event evidence; not reservoir evidence.
decision_reason: Accept as caveated spillover host.

checked_evidence_span: CDC/NEJM/prairie-dog model sources document 2003 US mpox outbreak in domesticated prairie dogs, human cases from direct contact, and experimental animal-to-animal transmission.
checked_evidence_location: cdc_132180_DS1.txt lines 29-43; NEJMoa032299.txt lines 44-57, 240-264; mpox_dog.txt lines 395-398, 430-432
caveat: Prairie dogs were outbreak amplification/transmission hosts in 2003 US context; not natural reservoir evidence.
decision_reason: Direct outbreak and experimental transmission support.

checked_evidence_span: CDC states Zika is primarily transmitted by Ae. aegypti/albopictus and that Ae. hensilli may contribute in Pacific Islands; Yap CHIKV paper notes Ae. hensilli was implicated as a Zika epidemic vector on Yap and lab studies support Zika vector status.
checked_evidence_location: CDC Zika transmission page lines 108-130; tpmd-article-p429.txt lines 340-349, 392-393, 478-479
caveat: Pacific Islands/Yap evidence; keep low-confidence candidate and do not upgrade to epidemic/main vector without primary Zika paper.
decision_reason: Official CDC plus secondary local support justifies low-confidence candidate row.

checked_evidence_span: Althouse et al. report high CHIKV seroprevalence in patas monkeys and estimate species-specific force of infection / R0 consistent with amplification in the sylvatic cycle.
caveat: Senegal/Kedougou sylvatic-cycle context; source does not establish continuous maintenance or reservoir status.
decision_reason: Direct species-level support for patas monkeys as sylvatic amplification hosts with non-reservoir caveat.

checked_evidence_span: Althouse et al. report high CHIKV seroprevalence in Guinea baboons and estimate species-specific force of infection / R0 consistent with amplification in the sylvatic cycle.
caveat: Senegal/Kedougou sylvatic-cycle context; source does not establish continuous maintenance or reservoir status.
decision_reason: Direct species-level support for Guinea baboons as sylvatic amplification hosts with non-reservoir caveat.

checked_evidence_span: Vourc'h et al. report CHIKV antibody detection in Macaca fascicularis after the Indian Ocean outbreak, but CHIKV RNA was not detected in tested sera.
caveat: Exposure evidence only; do not infer amplification, reservoir, or maintenance role.
decision_reason: Keep as source-check context only unless an evidence-only import path is added later.
